preamble: The shift from the voiced alveolar/dental stop [d] to the voiced dental fricative [ð] is a textbook example of spirantization and lenition. It occurs very frequently when the stop is placed between two vowels, causing it to weaken and allow continuous airflow.

examples.note: Begedkefat spirantization: post-vocalic /d/ → [ð] in Biblical Hebrew and Aramaic. The shift produced the historical dālet/ḏālet distinction.
Intervocalic /d/ spirantizes to the voiced dental approximant [ð̞] in all Spanish dialects.
Grammatical lenition (séimhiú): /d/ → [ð] in Old Irish, which further shifted to [ɣ] in most modern Goidelic environments.

In archaic Persian (up to the Mongolian era), /d/ spirantized to [ð] in post-vocalic position, producing the allophone [ð] beside underlying /d/. Early New Persian manuscripts record this variation. By the 13th century, [ð] reverted to [d] in most dialects.
Sogdian spirantized inherited /d/ to [ð] in all positions, including word-initially — a more thoroughgoing spirantization than Persian, which limited the shift to post-vocalic environments.

Kabyle spirantization: lax /d/ in intervocalic position weakens to the voiced dental fricative [ð]. Geminate /dd/ and post-nasal /nd/ resist spirantization. Kabyle is classified as a 'spirantizing' Berber language, contrasting with non-spirantizing varieties like Zenaga.
Middle Atlas Tamazight dialects (Ayt Ndhir area) show the same spirantization pattern as Kabyle: lax /d/ → [ð] in weak prosodic positions. Tense /dː/ and post-sonorant /nd/ remain as stops.

sources: Skjærvø, P. O. (2009). Middle Persian. In Windfuhr (ed.), The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
Kossmann, M. G. & Stroomer, H. J. (1997). Berber Phonology. In Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa. Eisenbrauns.
context: Post-vocalic and intervocalic positions; word-medial and word-final contexts
allophones_note: /d/ surfaces as [ð] in post-vocalic and intervocalic positions across Hebrew, Aramaic, Spanish, and Goidelic languages, while [d] is preserved in word-initial position.
